tokenizer: approx@3.7B/tok (tiktoken unavailable in env; offline pip install blocked)
notes: N=3 per arm/size, single machine, prod https://marigold-collab-web.vercel.app.

Generation-bound measurement: t0 stamped at end of a turn (post doc-create); the payload is then EMITTED as my own output tokens in the NEXT turn (full page typed into a heredoc for full-replace; only the ops JSON for patch); t1 stamped right after the write response. t1-t0 brackets model token-generation of the payload plus one harness round-trip + the write request. Generation dominates the full-replace arm.
The full-replace payload was genuinely re-generated (typed out) each rep, NOT echoed from the on-disk fixture via curl -d @file (that would incur ~0 generation time and misrepresent the cost).

Fixed per-measurement overhead (harness turn round-trip + curl + non-payload text) is ~7-10s and sits almost entirely in the patch arm's wall-clock (207-byte ops generate in <1s). This INFLATES patch_ms, making the reported speedup CONSERVATIVE; the pure generation gap is larger.

Both arms produce the SAME visible change: h1 setText -> 'Q3 revenue: $4.2M - updated' + appended

Update

Revision applied.

. setText target = first content element

mg-3d7f23d94e (the literal first data-marigold-id is
, but setText on it would wipe the doc). append target =
mg-a1dac1fc57 (afterend lands before , matching the full-replace insertion point).
Each arm ran on its own fresh pristine-seed quick doc (admin-token create, no rate limit). marigold-ids are deterministic content hashes, so identical across docs of the same seed.

Large full-replace emitted bytes drifted slightly per rep (106869/104758/105115 vs 102603-byte seed) because re-emitting ~100KB by hand introduced a few duplicated tables - itself the realistic 'full-replace can corrupt unchanged content' failure mode that patch structurally avoids. Figures are ACTUAL emitted sizes; large full_replace_bytes = median 105115 (range 104758-106869).

Wire time is minor: full-replace also uploads 30-100KB vs patch's 207 bytes, but over broadband that is tens of ms vs multi-second/multi-minute generation - the finding is generation-bound, as intended.
A real cl100k tokenizer would rate dense HTML markup at ~4.0-4.3 B/tok and the tiny ops near 3.7, so true token_reduction is slightly HIGHER than the byte-ratio approximation shown.
Docs left in place (not deleted), per instructions.
